title: Trapping Rain Water II
description: Given a 2D elevation map compute how much rainwater can be trapped

examples.explanation: 3x3 map, center (1,1) height=1. All paths to boundary go through height 3 cells. Any path: start 1, then 3 to boundary. Max along any path=3 (since 3>1). Min of maxes=3. Water=max(0,3-1)=2. All other cells boundary or height 3. Total=2.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(m²n²)
approaches.summary: For each cell, compute trapped water by finding the minimum of the maximum heights along all paths to the boundary, which is inefficient. This method involves checking all possible paths for every cell, making it impractical for larger maps.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Trapping Rain Water II
ladders.desc: Complete O(m²n²) solution checking all paths for each cell to compute trapped water.

explanation.prose: This brute force approach solves the full 2D rainwater trapping problem by examining every possible path from each interior cell to the boundary. For each cell (i,j), we need to find the maximum height encountered along every possible path to the map boundary, then take the minimum of these maximums across all paths. This minimum represents the highest water level that can be contained at that cell without leaking. The trapped water at cell (i,j) is then max(0, min_max_height - height[i][j]). The intuition is that water at a cell is limited by the lowest 'bottleneck' along any escape path to the boundary - if there's any path where the maximum height is low, water will leak out that way. We must check ALL possible paths because we don't know in advance which path provides the lowest bottleneck.

connectsTo.reason: This uses the path checking subroutine repeatedly

commonMistakes: Not checking ALL paths (greedy approaches fail)
integer overflow with large maps. Real pattern: nested loops over cells
algorithm: 1) For each interior cell (i,j), initialize min_max = infinity
2) Generate all possible paths from (i,j) to any boundary cell using DFS/BFS
3) For each path, find the maximum height along that path
6) Sum water across all cells. Time: O(m²n²) because for each of O(mn) cells, we check O(mn) paths in worst case. Space: O(

examples.trace: 3x3 map, center (1,1) height=1. All paths to boundary go through height 3 cells. Any path: start 1, then 3 to boundary. Max along any path=3 (since 3>1). Min of maxes=3. Water=max(0,3-1)=2. All other cells boundary or height 3. Total=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Minimum Maximum Height Along All Paths from a Cell
ladders.desc: For a given cell, compute the minimum of maximum heights across all paths to boundary.

explanation.prose: This subroutine is the core computational step in the brute force approach. Given a starting cell (i,j) in a 2D height map, we need to find min_max = minimum over all possible paths to any boundary cell of (maximum height along that path). The intuition: water at a cell will leak out along the path with the lowest 'bottleneck' (lowest maximum height). By finding the minimum of these bottleneck values across ALL paths, we determine the maximum water level possible at that cell. Algorithm: 1) Initialize min_max = INF. 2) Use DFS/BFS to explore all paths from (i,j) to boundary cells. 3) For each complete path, compute path_max = maximum height value along that path. 4) Update min_max = min(min_max, path_max). 5) Return min_max. We must explore ALL paths without early termination because a shorter path might have higher max, while a longer path might have lower max. Time: O(mn) in worst case as we may explore all cells in the map for one starting cell. Space: O(mn) for recursion stack. Connection to L2: This implements the core min-of-max concept.

connectsTo.reason: This function is called for each interior cell in the full solution

commonMistakes: not tracking visited cells properly (infinite loops)
incorrect boundary detection. Real pattern: DFS with backtracking

examples.trace: From (1,1): Explore 4 directions. Path1: (1,1)→(1,0) height3→(0,0) height1 boundary: max=3. Path2: (1,1)→(1,0) boundary: max=3. Path3: (1,1)→(0,1) height4→(0,0): max=4. Path4: (1,1)→(0,1)→(0,2): max=4. Path5: (1,1)→(1,2) height1→(0,2): max=3. Path6: (1,1)→(1,2)→(2,2) height2 boundary: max=2. Path7: (1,1)→(2,1) height1→(2,0) height2 boundary: max=2. Path8: (1,1)→(2,1)→(2,2): max=2. Minimum of maxes=2.
From center (1,1): All adjacent cells height=3. Any path: (1,1) height1 → neighbor height3 → boundary. Maximum along any path=3 (since 3>1). All paths have max=3. Minimum of maxes=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Minimum of Maximum Heights Concept
ladders.desc: Understanding that water level at a cell equals the minimum bottleneck height across all escape paths.

explanation.prose: This is the central insight for rainwater trapping: For any interior cell, the maximum water height it can hold is determined by the minimum value among the maximum heights encountered along ALL possible paths to the map boundary. Why? Imagine water at a cell: it will try to flow out to the boundary. Along any given path, water cannot rise above the highest point on that path (the bottleneck). If water rose higher, it would spill over that high point. However, water will leak out along the path with the LOWEST bottleneck - that's the easiest escape route. Therefore, the actual water level is limited by the minimum of these bottleneck values across all paths. Mathematically: water_level[i][j] = min over all paths P from (i,j) to boundary of (max over cells in P of height). Then trapped water = max(0, water_level - height[i][j]). This min-of-max principle transforms the physical problem into a computational one. It works because water seeks the path of least resistance (lowest high point). The brute force approach directly implements this by checking all paths. Time/space for just this concept: O(1) to understand, but implementing requires path exploration. Connection to L3: This builds on understanding paths and maximum tracking.

connectsTo.reason: This concept is what the subroutine computes

commonMistakes: not considering all paths. Real pattern: Two-level optimization: inner max along path

examples.input: Cell with height 2, two paths: Path1 max=4, Path2 max=3
examples.trace: Path1 bottleneck=4 (water would spill if >4). Path2 bottleneck=3 (water would spill if >3). Water will leak out Path2 if level exceeds 3. Therefore maximum sustainable level = min(4,3)=3. Water trapped = max(0,3-2)=1.

examples.input: Cell height 1, three paths with maxes 5,6,4
examples.trace: Bottlenecks: 5,6,4. Minimum=4. Water cannot exceed 4 or it leaks via path with max=4. Trapped water = max(0,4-1)=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Maximum Height Along a Path in Grid
ladders.desc: Given a path in 2D grid, compute the maximum height value along that path.

explanation.prose: This intermediate concept is essential for the brute force approach: we need to analyze individual paths. A path is a sequence of adjacent cells (up/down/left/right) from start to boundary. The maximum height along a path is simply the largest height value among all cells visited in that path. Why is this important? In rainwater trapping, each path's maximum acts as a bottleneck - water cannot rise above this value along that path. Algorithm: 1) Initialize max_height = -INF. 2) For each cell in the path (including start and all intermediate cells): 3) Update max_height = max(max_height, height[cell]). 4) Return max_height. The intuition: as water flows along a path, the highest point determines where it would spill over. This is a simple reduction operation over a sequence. Time: O(L) where L is path length. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This uses basic comparison operations.

connectsTo.reason: This computes the inner 'max' in the min-of-max concept

commonMistakes: off-by-one errors. Real pattern: Iterate through path list

examples.trace: Cell1 height=2, max=2. Cell2 height=1, max remains 2. Cell3 height=2, max remains 2. Final max=2.
Start height=1, max=1. Next height=4, max=4. Next height=3, max remains 4. Final=4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Grid Traversal and Adjacency Checking
ladders.desc: Moving between adjacent cells in a 2D grid and checking boundary conditions.

explanation.prose: This fundamental operation involves navigating a 2D grid by moving to adjacent cells (up, down, left, right) and determining when we've reached the boundary. In the rainwater problem, we need to explore paths, which requires this basic movement capability. For a cell at position (i,j) in an m×n grid: - Up: (i-1,j) if i>0 - Down: (i+1,j) if i<m-1 - Left: (i,j-1) if j>0 - Right: (i,j+1) if j<n-1 A boundary cell is one where i==0 or i==m-1 or j==0 or j==n-1. The intuition: water flows to the edge of the map, so our paths must reach these boundary cells. This operation is the building block for generating paths. Algorithm to get neighbors: 1) Initialize empty list neighbors. 2) Check each direction: if valid (within bounds), add to neighbors. 3) Return neighbors. Time: O(1) per cell. Space: O(1). Connection to L5: This uses array indexing.
commonMistakes: incorrect boundary detection. Real pattern: Direction vectors [(0,1)

examples.trace: i=1,j=1. Up: i-1=0≥0 → (0,1). Down: i+1=2≤2 → (2,1). Left: j-1=0≥0 → (1,0). Right: j+1=2≤2 → (1,2). Boundary? i=1 not 0 or 2, j=1 not 0 or 2 → not boundary.
i=0,j=0. Up: i-1=-1<0 → invalid. Down: i+1=1≤1 → (1,0). Left: j-1=-1<0 → invalid. Right: j+1=1≤1 → (0,1). Boundary? i==0 → yes boundary.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — 2D Array Indexing and Basic Comparisons
ladders.desc: Accessing elements in a 2D grid and performing simple min/max operations.

explanation.prose: This is the most fundamental prerequisite: working with 2D arrays (matrices) and basic comparison operations. A 2D array is indexed as grid[i][j] where i is row index (0 to m-1) and j is column index (0 to n-1). We need to access height values at specific positions. Basic comparisons involve finding minimum or maximum of values: min(a,b) returns the smaller value, max(a,b) returns the larger. For multiple values, we can compute running min/max: initialize result, then for each new value, update result = min(result, new_val) or max(result, new_val). Intuition: In rainwater trapping, we constantly compare heights to find bottlenecks. This foundation is essential because the entire algorithm revolves around comparing height values across cells. Time: O(1) per access/comparison. Space: O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This enables height lookup during traversal

commonMistakes: incorrect min/max initialization. Real pattern: grid[i][j] access

approaches.name: BFS with Multi-Source Initialization O(mn)
approaches.summary: Initialize a BFS queue with all boundary cells and use a visited set to propagate, computing water trapping by comparing heights with the current boundary level. This method avoids log factors by using a queue but may require careful height tracking.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Trapping Rain Water II with BFS Multi-Source Initialization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using BFS starting from boundary cells to compute trapped rainwater in a 2D elevation map.

explanation.prose: This solution implements the BFS with multi-source initialization approach for the 2D trapping rainwater problem. The intuition is that water can only be trapped if there's a boundary of higher cells forming a 'bowl'. By starting from the lowest boundary cells and propagating inward, we can determine how much water each cell can hold relative to the current boundary level. The algorithm initializes a min-heap (or priority queue) with all boundary cells, marking them as visited. Then it performs BFS: at each step, it pops the cell with minimum height, updates the current water level to the maximum of its previous value and the popped cell's height, and processes unvisited neighbors. For each neighbor, if its height is less than the current water level, water can be trapped there equal to the difference, which is added to the total. The neighbor is then pushed into the heap with its original height (not the water level). This ensures we always process cells in increasing order of effective height, similar to water flowing from low to high barriers.

connectsTo.reason: by implementing the full BFS traversal

timeComplexity.note: ) if using a heap, but O(mn) with a queue if we track heights carefully—here we'll use a heap for clarity

spaceComplexity.note: for the heap and visited set

commonMistakes: not updating the water level correctly
forgetting to mark cells as visited when pushing to heap
explanation.codePattern: Initialize heap with boundary cells, visited set, water_level = 0, total = 0

examples.trace: Input: 3x6 grid. Step 1: Initialize heap with all boundary cells (18 cells): coordinates and heights. Visited set has these cells. Water level = 0, total = 0. Step 2: Pop cell with min height: (0,0) height 1. Update water_level = max(0,1) = 1. Process neighbors: (0,1) height 4 (not visited, mark visited, height 4 >= 1, no water, push with height 4), (1,0) height 3 (not visited, mark visited, height 3 >= 1, no water, push with height 3). Step 3: Pop next min: (0,2) height 3 (from boundary). Water_level = max(1,3) = 3. Process neighbors: (0,3) height 1 (not visited, mark visited, height 1 < 3, total += 2, push with height 1), (1,2) height 1 (not visited, mark visited, height 1 < 3, total += 2, push with height 1). Total now 4. Continue BFS: eventually process all cells. Final total = 4.

Input: 5x5 grid. Step 1: Initialize heap with boundary cells (16 cells, all height 3 except corners? Actually all boundary cells have height 3). Visited set has these. Water_level = 0, total = 0. Step 2: Pop cell with min height: any boundary cell height 3. Water_level = 3. Process neighbors: inner cells. For example, pop (1,1) height 2: not visited, mark visited, height 2 < 3, total += 1, push with height 2. Similarly for all inner cells: there are 9 inner cells forming a 3x3 block with heights: center 1, others 2. Water_level remains 3. For center (2,2) height 1: total += 2. Total: 8 cells with height 2 add 8*1 = 8, center adds 2, total = 10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Water Level Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement BFS starting from boundary cells to propagate and compute water trapping using a priority queue.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine is the core BFS traversal that processes cells to determine trapped water. The intuition is that water trapping depends on the lowest boundary height surrounding a cell. By using a min-heap, we always process the cell with the smallest height first, effectively simulating water filling from the lowest point outward. The algorithm: Initialize a min-heap with all boundary cells (each as a tuple of height, row, col) and a visited set containing these cells. Set current water level to 0 and total water to 0. While the heap is not empty: pop the cell with minimum height. Update the water level to the maximum of the current water level and the popped cell's height—this represents the lowest height that water cannot flow over at this stage. For each of the four neighbors (up, down, left, right): if the neighbor is within bounds and not visited, mark it as visited. If the neighbor's height is less than the current water level, it can trap water: add (water level - neighbor's height) to the total. Push the neighbor into the heap with its original height (not the water level). This ensures we process cells in order of increasing effective barrier height. This feeds into L0 as the main algorithmic loop.

timeComplexity.note: ) due to heap operations for each cell

spaceComplexity.note: for the heap and visited set

commonMistakes: pushing neighbors with updated water height instead of original height
not updating the water level correctly

examples.input: Grid: [[1,2],[2,1]], start from boundary cells: (0,0) h=1, (0,1) h=2, (1,0) h=2, (1,1) is interior.
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize heap with boundary cells: [(1,0,0), (2,0,1), (2,1,0)], visited has these. Water_level=0, total=0. Step 2: Pop min: (1,0,0). Water_level = max(0,1)=1. Process neighbors: (0,1) already visited; (1,0) already visited. Step 3: Pop next: (2,0,1). Water_level = max(1,2)=2. Process neighbors: (0,0) visited; (1,1) not visited, mark visited, height=1 < 2, total += 1, push (1,1,1) with height 1. Step 4: Pop (1,1,1). Water_level = max(2,1)=2. Process neighbors: all visited. Total remains 1? Wait, but output should be 0 because water leaks? Actually in this grid, boundary heights are 1,2,2 and interior 1, so water might trap? Let's compute: boundary min is 1, but interior is 1, so no water trapped. In trace, total became 1 due to error: when processing (1,1), water_level is 2, height is 1, so difference 1 added, but this is incorrect because water would flow out through lower boundary. The algorithm should yield 0. Let's correct: In step 3, when popping (2,0,1), water_level=2, neighbor (1,1) height 1 < 2, but actually water cannot be trapped because there's a boundary cell (0,0) with height 1 lower, so water would flow out. The algorithm as described might overcount—this highlights a common mistake: the algorithm requires careful initialization to avoid this. For this example, with correct implementation, total=0.

Step 1: Initialize heap with boundary cells (8 cells, all height 3), visited set. Water_level=0, total=0. Step 2: Pop any boundary cell, e.g., (3,0,0). Water_level=3. Process neighbors: (0,1) visited? Actually (0,1) is boundary and visited; (1,0) boundary visited. Step 3: Pop another boundary cell until we get to interior. Eventually pop a cell that allows processing (1,1). When (1,1) is processed: it's not visited initially? Actually in initialization, only boundary cells are visited, so (1,1) is not visited. When a neighbor boundary cell processes it: mark visited, height=1 < 3, total += 2, push (1,1,1) with height 1. Total=2. Continue BFS: no more unvisited cells. Final total=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Multi-Source BFS Initialization from Boundary
ladders.desc: Initialize BFS with all boundary cells to simulate water filling from edges inward.

explanation.prose: This core logic involves setting up the BFS by adding all boundary cells of the grid to the priority queue. The intuition is that water can only be trapped if it's enclosed by higher boundaries; by starting from the edges, we ensure we consider the lowest points where water might escape. The algorithm: For a grid of size m x n, iterate through all cells. If a cell is on the boundary (i.e., row is 0 or m-1 or col is 0 or n-1), add it to a min-heap as a tuple (height, row, col), and add its coordinates to a visited set. This initialization captures all cells that form the initial 'barrier'. The heap will process cells in increasing height order, which is crucial because water flows from low to high barriers. This step ensures that we always have the smallest boundary height to compare against interior cells. Time complexity for initialization is O(m+n) since there are approximately 2(m+n) boundary cells, but we iterate all cells O(mn) to identify them—so O(mn). This feeds into L1 as the setup for BFS traversal.

spaceComplexity.note: for the heap and visited set at this stage

commonMistakes: forgetting to mark boundary cells as visited
explanation.codePattern: m, n = len(grid), len(grid[0]); heap = []; visited = set(); for i in range(m): for j in range(n): if i == 0 or i == m-1 or j == 0 or j == n-1: heappush(heap, (grid[i][j], i, j)); visited

examples.output: Heap initial: [(1,0,0), (2,0,1), (3,1,0), (4,1,1)] since all cells are boundary in 2x2.
examples.trace: Step 1: m=2, n=2. Iterate cells: (0,0) height 1: boundary (row 0), push (1,0,0) to heap, add to visited. (0,1) height 2: boundary (row 0), push (2,0,1), add visited. (1,0) height 3: boundary (row m-1=1), push (3,1,0), add visited. (1,1) height 4: boundary (row m-1 and col n-1), push (4,1,1), add visited. Final heap has 4 elements in min-heap order.

Step 1: m=3, n=3. Boundary cells: first row (0,0)-(0,2): heights 5,6,7; last row (2,0)-(2,2): heights 2,3,4; first column (1,0): height 8; last column (1,2): height 1. Note: (1,1) height 9 is interior, not boundary. So heap gets 8 elements. Visited set has these 8 coordinates.

ladders.title: Building Block — Priority Queue (Min-Heap) for Cell Processing
ladders.desc: Use a min-heap to always process the cell with the smallest height first.

explanation.prose: This building block involves implementing a priority queue, specifically a min-heap, to manage cells during BFS. The intuition is that in water trapping, the limiting factor is the lowest barrier height; by processing cells in increasing height order, we simulate water filling from the lowest point upward. The algorithm: Represent each cell as a tuple (height, row, col) in the heap. The heap is a data structure that allows efficient insertion and extraction of the minimum element. Operations: push (heappush) adds a cell to the heap while maintaining heap order; pop (heappop) removes and returns the cell with the smallest height. During BFS, we repeatedly pop the minimum cell, update the water level, and push neighbors. This ensures that we always consider the current lowest boundary when evaluating water trapping. Time complexity for heap operations is O(log N) per push/pop, where N is the number of cells in the heap, up to O(mn). This feeds into L2 as the mechanism for ordered processing.

spaceComplexity.note: for storing cells in the heap

commonMistakes: using a max-heap instead of min-heap
not maintaining the heap property after pushes

examples.trace: Step 1: Heap has elements: (3,0,0), (1,0,1), (2,1,0) in heap order (min-heap ensures smallest at root). Step 2: heapq.heappop() returns (1,0,1) as it's the smallest height 1. Step 3: After pop, heap reorganizes: remaining (2,1,0) and (3,0,0), with (2,1,0) as new min. Step 4: Next pop returns (2,1,0). Step 5: Final pop returns (3,0,0).

examples.input: Push sequence: push (5,0,0), push (2,1,1), push (3,2,2), then pop repeatedly.
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with empty heap. Step 2: heapq.heappush(heap, (5,0,0)) -> heap [(5,0,0)]. Step 3: heapq.heappush(heap, (2,1,1)) -> heap adjusts to [(2,1,1), (5,0,0)]. Step 4: heapq.heappush(heap, (3,2,2)) -> heap [(2,1,1), (5,0,0), (3,2,2)]. Step 5: heapq.heappop() returns (2,1,1). Heap now [(3,2,2), (5,0,0)]. Step 6: heapq.heappop() returns (3,2,2). Heap now [(5,0,0)]. Step 7: heapq.heappop() returns (5,0,0).

testCases.expected: Pop order: 5,5,5 (any order for ties, but min-heap gives one)
Pop order: 2, then after push, pop 1, then 3
Pop order: 6, then after push, pop 5, then 7

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Grid Traversal with Neighbor Checking
ladders.desc: Iterate through grid cells and access their four orthogonal neighbors.

explanation.prose: This basic operation involves navigating a 2D grid by iterating through rows and columns, and for each cell, checking its four adjacent neighbors (up, down, left, right). The intuition is that in many grid-based problems, including water trapping, the behavior of a cell depends on its neighbors. The algorithm: Use nested loops to iterate over each cell with indices i from 0 to m-1 and j from 0 to n-1. For a given cell (i,j), define its neighbors as (i-1,j), (i+1,j), (i,j-1), (i,j+1). Before accessing a neighbor, check if it's within the grid bounds (0 <= ni < m and 0 <= nj < n) to avoid index errors. This operation is fundamental for BFS where we propagate from a cell to its neighbors. Time complexity for traversing all cells is O(mn), and checking neighbors is O(1) per cell. This feeds into L3 as part of processing cells in the heap.
explanation.codePattern: directions = [(-1,0), (1,0), (0,-1), (0,1)]; for i in range(m): for j in range(n): # process cell (i,j); for di, dj in directions: ni, nj = i+di, j+dj; if 0 <= ni < m and 0 <= nj < n: # access grid[ni][nj]

examples.output: Neighbors: (1,0) height 3, (0,1) height 2; ( -1,0) and (0,-1) out of bounds.
examples.trace: Step 1: Cell (0,0) has i=0, j=0. Step 2: Check neighbor up: i-1 = -1, j=0 -> out of bounds, skip. Step 3: Check neighbor down: i+1=1, j=0 -> within bounds, neighbor is (1,0) with height 3. Step 4: Check neighbor left: i=0, j-1=-1 -> out of bounds, skip. Step 5: Check neighbor right: i=0, j+1=1 -> within bounds, neighbor is (0,1) with height 2.

Step 1: Cell (1,1) is at center. Step 2: Neighbor up: (0,1) height 6, within bounds. Step 3: Neighbor down: (2,1) height 3, within bounds. Step 4: Neighbor left: (1,0) height 8, within bounds. Step 5: Neighbor right: (1,2) height 1, within bounds.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — 2D Grid Representation and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand how to represent and access elements in a 2D grid using row and column indices.

explanation.prose: This foundation concept covers the basics of working with a 2D grid, which is a matrix or array of arrays. The intuition is that a 2D grid is a common data structure for problems involving spatial relationships, like elevation maps. A grid is typically represented as a list of lists in Python, where the outer list contains rows, and each inner list contains the elements in that row. Indexing is done with two indices: grid[i][j] accesses the element at row i and column j, where i starts from 0 at the top row and increases downward, and j starts from 0 at the left column and increases rightward. The size of the grid is defined by the number of rows m = len(grid) and number of columns n = len(grid[0]) assuming all rows have the same length. To iterate through all cells, use nested loops: for i in range(m): for j in range(n): cell = grid[i][j]. This allows accessing each cell's value, which in water trapping represents height. Time complexity for traversing the entire grid is O(mn). This feeds into L4 as the basis for neighbor checking.

spaceComplexity.note: for iteration, but O(mn) to store the grid

commonMistakes: assuming the grid is square when it might be rectangular
explanation.codePattern: grid = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]; m = len(grid); n = len(grid[0]); for i in range(m): for j in range(n): print(grid[i][j])
